videos.text: This is a beautiful blood orange. And I want to explain to you very, very quickly the concept of abstraction. So here's a little whistle graph. Would you like to come down the stairs? I don't want to 'cause I'm too scared. Woo! So that's a whistle graph. And I could do that very incorrectly. I could do it wrong. I could be like, would you go down, like, to the-- Woo! And you would say, oh, Jeffrey, that's not a good whistle graph. That's a bad whistle graph. And you'll be right, that's incorrect. Whereas this is correct. But what about abstraction? What about abstract painting? That would be something like this. Would you like to go down the stairs? I don't want to 'cause I'm too scared. Woo! So notice how I spatially, like, the marks are spatially weird, but I did get some things correct. And that's why people often argue about abstract painting. So basically that's abstraction, and that's just wrong.

videos.desc: @eian_b this should help you understand the use value of abstraction! Without ♾ there’d be no computers, and w/o 🙂, no cartoons, or alphabet❗️
videos.text: Question. Abstract art is just an excuse for non-talented people to do art. Well, let's talk about abstraction in mathematics and see if abstraction is an excuse for non-talented people to do mathematics. So if you have one thing, right, you can have one thing and then two things, right? And we use symbols for those things, like one and two, right? And then you can have three things, right? Now that's very concrete, right? That's concrete mathematics. I can have one, two or three sticks. But what if I want to have infinity sticks, right? Infinity amount of sticks. Nobody has ever seen infinity amount of sticks. So we have a special symbol, an abstraction, in order to do things with something that nobody has ever seen. Now there's some things in art that people have also never seen. Those things are things like circles, triangles, and even a smiley face.

videos.desc: Reply to @acspy72 here is the difference between a drawing, doodle, and sketch! ✒️➿
videos.text: Now, the first thing you need to understand is what a drawing actually is. And a drawing, let's use this mountain do as an example, a drawing is not just a little mark like that, but a drawing is actually a set of marks that have some kind of ordering principle to them, a set of marks that have a pattern. And that's a drawing, but any old animal can make a drawing. For example, if you have a little kitty and it's making a trail and you're trying to follow the trail, well then that cat is making a drawing with its paw prints, but it's not necessarily a doodle or a sketch. Now, a doodle is a type of drawing that you make, the type of pattern of marks that you make when you don't have any ideas and are looking for an idea. Oh, I found a cool idea right here. And a sketch is something that you might do to develop that idea a little bit further.

videos.desc: ✒️🕳 scary emotions
videos.text: Sometimes people have a lot of really scary emotions inside and those emotions can express themselves in different ways. A lot of people choose to draw or to smush clay or something like that and shape it into different things when they have those emotions. But some people never learned how to do that and so they start to smush things or push things or press things that might have emotions themselves and that's not really good because then what happens is other people will get upset and then they'll have a lot of strong emotions and they won't know how to express those emotions either and so they'll start to express them in different places. Places that art shouldn't usually go and that can be a really big problem but it's also okay that that happens.

videos.desc: Reply to @herrokitty2000 this is what i think of geccing ✒️☺️ #geccing #artschool #drawing #edutok #100gecs
videos.text: Gekking comes from the band 100geks, and when 100geks found some graffiti on a wall that said 100geks, they're like, "What the heck does that mean?" And a lot of their music goes, "Gek, Gek, Gek, Gek, Gek, Gek, Gek, Gek, Gek, Gek." And so the graffiti they found on the wall was a little bit like an accident, an accidental find. And the Gekking, "Gek, Gek, Gek, Gek, Gek, Gek," is a little bit like a glitch, an accidental sound. It happens when you turn an accident back around and say, "Oh, I really appreciate accidents, and I want to create a whole art form out of the aesthetics of accidents." That's basically what Gekking is. And accidents occur in other kinds of art, too. For example, in painting, if you leave a drip and the drip drops down on the canvas, that is also a kind of Gekking.

is also a kind of Gekking.

videos.desc: How to draw more confidently and more comfortably! #edutok #drawing #art #artschool #lineart ✒️☺️
videos.text: A lot of people, when they first start to draw, they think in terms of the tip of the pen, and they imagine, and keep their imagination right where the tip of the pen is. And so when they draw, you'll see them going back and forth, like this, trying desperately to shape a line. But if you think about the pen more like driving a car, and you just drive along at the same speed, making your turns, trying to arrive at some future destination, then not only will you seem more confident, but you'll also be a lot more comfy.

segments.text: A lot of people, when they first start to draw, they think in terms of the tip of the pen,
and they imagine, and keep their imagination right where the tip of the pen is.
And so when they draw, you'll see them going back and forth, like this, trying desperately
to shape a line.
But if you think about the pen more like driving a car, and you just drive along at the same
speed, making your turns, trying to arrive at some future destination, then not only
will you seem more confident, but you'll also be a lot more comfy.

videos.desc: I don't need a portfolio to show my work to people. 🙂✒️🎶 #portfolio #art #artschool #drawing #artist
videos.text: One of the coolest things about whistle graphs is that they're memorizable. So one day, if someone says, "Hey, you're an artist, let me see your work. "I don't have to pull out my phone. "All I have to do is something like this." ♪ Well, well, well ♪ ♪ Look who it is ♪ ♪ Crawling in the corner of the room ♪ And then I'd be like, that's one of my pieces. What do you think about that picture? And I don't have to show them an image of a painting or anything.

videos.desc: College level media theory about TikTok, Google, and the future of the Internet. #tiktok #google #internet #mediatheory #lineart ✒️💭
videos.text: Hey guys, it's WhistlerGraph. I want to talk about some college-level media theory. So if Google was the interface of the Internet of the 90s and the 10s, you know, the text box, it assumed that you knew what to search for. But most people actually don't really know what they want. And so, TikTokers are basically human information clerks. We mostly tell you what to Google. And it's the new front door of Gen Z's Internet, soon to be everyone else's.

segments.text: Hey guys, it's WhistlerGraph. I want to talk about some college-level media theory.
So if Google was the interface of the Internet of the 90s and the 10s, you know, the text box,
it assumed that you knew what to search for.
But most people actually don't really know what they want.
And so, TikTokers are basically human information clerks.
We mostly tell you what to Google.
And it's the new front door of Gen Z's Internet, soon to be everyone else's.

videos.desc: how to change ur ink! #fountainpen #ink #art
videos.text: It's time to change the ink in my fountain pen. Taking off the cap, and taking the cap off my pen. Unscrewing the nib and the cartridge. Pushing down the piston, dipping it in the ink all the way. And sucking that ink up by screwing the piston back in. Now we have to wipe off the whole excess from the nib. Put the back back on, put the cap back on, give it a little whip. And we're ready to go.

videos.desc: What's your default drawing mode? … I bet you know mine by now! ✒️🙂
videos.text: I want to tell you about drawing modes. Now, these are ways that you can draw. So the first one is doodling. You can doodle anything you want, as long as you have no idea what you're doing. Next is sketching. Sketching is for getting a rough idea of what you might want to do. Now, writing, that's for sending verbal commands and telling people what to do. Tracing is when you already have something down and you need to follow along. Diagramming is how you make that money. That's how you go from point A to point B and make all the dollars and keep track of things. Whistle graphing is different though. That's for leisure and enjoyment and it goes like this. (whistling)

videos.desc: Answer to @unh0lyboy ✒️🥰 #edutok #artform #computerscience #programming
videos.text: One of the ways that computer science people like to study programming language design is they create something that is often called a "toy" language and this is something to mess around with that won't mess up the real world. Now I'm a contemporary artist and so I decided to create a toy art form, which I call whistlegrafing, in order to better understand all the different art forms and how they come to be.

videos.desc: Answer to @malekpitney here's an in-depth composition tutorial! #arttutorial #poppy #fountainpen #learn 🌺
videos.text: I'm going to take a break from my lunch salad here to answer this interesting question of how to actually compose a whistle graph. Now the step one is to soak up inspiration. I'm outside my house right now, and as you can see there are these beautiful poppy seed flowers that are growing and I'm kind of inspired by them actually. We can take a closer look here. Look how nice these are. They're really cool. Check it out you guys. And then look there's all these buds that are about to pop. Right? This is going to be a whole little patch of poppy flowers in my backyard. And so let's say I want to write a whistle graph about this. I have my inspo over there in the corner and so now step two is to sketch and listen. So I'm going to start sketching. One, two, one, two, three. Do, do, do, do, do, do, do, do, do. Dun, dun, dun, dun, dun. I kind of like that. Dun, dun, dun, dun. And what I can do is I can turn that into like a score. So step three is to score and try to find a tight relationship between the drawing and actually having the lines. I'm going to sketch this poppy a little bit more actually. Let me see. So it kind of looks like that. And then it has all these flowery lines coming out. But these are kind of like, they're kind of chaotic. So I'd have to figure out a way of turning these into steps. So like one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight. You know, maybe eight times I would loop over them and then I would add something in the center to finish the poppy. So I have to turn that into steps, and so at this point I might make a new page And start turning that into steps, so what if we had dent? dent dent dent dent dent dent dent dent dent dent dent dent and then this would continue on dent dent dent dent dent dent So you have all these little undulations, and maybe I could say that's going to be times eight so now I can say like is there that's two a flower That right is There a flower that right and you start to link together the words and the lines And that's basically how you make a whistle graph and then once you kind of finish that you have to record it So everyone can see it and then you have to repeat and memorize it So you really know it by heart and that's basically how it's done

And that's basically how you make a whistle graph and then once you kind of finish that you have to record it
So everyone can see it and then you have to repeat and memorize it
So you really know it by heart and that's basically how it's done

videos.desc: logos vs. whistlegraphs
videos.text: Logos are complicated. They're fascist and idealistic. There's only one way to do them. There's no sound or poetry and they enforce a corporate hierarchy. Whistle graphs on the other hand are memorizable. Everyone in your company can do it in their own style and not only that, but it includes music, visuals, and verbal meaning. It builds solidarity in your company.

in your company.

videos.desc: analyzing my own #artsymath videos ✒️➕🎵 #art #math #theory #mediatheory
videos.text: One plus two equals three. So artsy math is by far one of my most popular TikTok videos. And at first I didn't really understand why it was popular, but if you look at the sound, you'll realize it's pretty much all six, seven, eight year old kids who do edit and who love it. Kind of creep me out at first, but then I realized that kids who are that age are actually learning arithmetic in school. And to them, this video is kind of politically radical. It's like going way outside the bounds of what they're allowed to do every day. And so in that sense, it's kind of like a useful form of politics.

videos.desc: scary emotions (with subtitles) on the occasion of this drawing being sold ✒️🥺
videos.text: Sometimes people have a lot of really scary emotions inside and those emotions can express themselves in different ways. A lot of people choose to draw or to smush clay or something like that and shape it into different things when they have those emotions but some people never learned how to do that and so they start to smush things or push things or press things that might have emotions themselves and that's not really good because then what happens is other people will get upset and then they'll have a lot of strong emotions and they won't know how to express those emotions either and so they'll start to express them in different places. Places that art shouldn't usually go and that can be a really big a really big problem but it's also okay that that happens.

videos.desc: make lines that look like how you think ✒️☺️ #painting #learnontiktok #abstractart #lecture
videos.text: If you want to do interesting abstract art and do it well, then there's a few things that you should know. First of all, you can't just go crazy, like, because if you just go crazy, then you end up actually doing basically the same thing over and over again. For example, you might do a roving line, or you know, you might be making lots of dots, or we might be doing a lot of BF back and forth, right? So you have to develop your own language, but how do you actually do that with abstract art? Well, one of the things you can do is go a little crazy, like that, and then take care of how crazy you went, and you do that quite a lot, and you'll eventually learn how to use your hand better, and actually figure out sort of what your hand in your eyes is trying to tell you. And it doesn't matter if it looks like anything, what matters is how it feels to you and the viewer.

segments.text: If you want to do interesting abstract art and do it well, then there's a few things that you should know.
First of all, you can't just go crazy, like, because if you just go crazy, then you end up actually doing basically the same thing over and over again.
For example, you might do a roving line, or you know, you might be making lots of dots, or we might be doing a lot of BF back and forth, right?
So you have to develop your own language, but how do you actually do that with abstract art?
Well, one of the things you can do is go a little crazy, like that, and then take care of how crazy you went, and you do that quite a lot,
and you'll eventually learn how to use your hand better, and actually figure out sort of what your hand in your eyes is trying to tell you.
And it doesn't matter if it looks like anything, what matters is how it feels to you and the viewer.

Factory cookie cutter personalities we must break free from the states that we're in. Spinning away I hear a bird. Spinning away I hear a bird. And parameters of it can even be bent. Factory cookie cutter personalities we must break free from the states that we're in. Spinning away I hear a bird. It's parametric.

And I have one discord, which is the whistograph discord.
And the whistograph discord, I invited the NFT people to, but then the whistograph mods
kicked them all out.
And so I'm encouraging the whistograph discord to be really anti-NFTs in a way.
You know, I just want them to do what they want to do.
But then on the crypto discord, I'm encouraging them to be really pro-NFTs.
And I think that eventually the discords are going to fight each other, you guys, and it's
going to be really crazy.
And I think we're going to learn a lot about the war.

videos.text: Well, perhaps I can convince you to love whistle graphs if you hate whistle graphs. So one of the most interesting things about whistle graphs as an art form is "Would you like to come down those stairs?" I don't want to because I'm too scared. Is that they're memorizable. So one day when Geoffrey here, the master whistle grapher is super super old and super super about to die with really long hair and I have a little lassie sitting on my lap like my grand kid, I'll be able to pull out a pen and show them all my work and perform for them all my whistle graphs that I've ever written and I don't have to pull out some old computer, I don't have to take them to a museum, all the work will be stored in here and if I forget anything then I can refer to the instructions. So whistle graphs are kind of cool, formally speaking, as an art form just because it's kind of like taking painting off of the canvas.

videos.text: Yeah, whistle graphs are a made-up art form. Pretty much every art form is made up at some point. And if you use an existing art form with an existing method or technique, then usually you're going to produce an already existing emotional effect in the viewer. So if you want to come up with something, a new kind of feeling that a viewer can have, if you want to make the viewer feel new things, then you have to come up with new methods. I was trapped, and I fell down. I broke my head and died.

videos.desc: what talking to me about computers is like 💙✒️🎶
videos.text: I have this piece that like it's like a square and you click and drag and then you can move these things around and create these little drawings, right? And then on the bottom in every pixel I'm actually gonna encode the steps to reproduce the drawing until the progress bar fills up and then I'm gonna store these steps in the image but also these numbers are gonna be stored on chain so I'm gonna have redundancy because visually these are decodable into the steps so there's gonna be a player program that can load the images and play them back and also the data for that is gonna be stored on chain too so even if you don't have the image but you just have the chain data you can use the player to reconstruct the image

segments.text: I have this piece that like it's like a square and you click and drag and then
you can move these things around and create these little drawings, right? And
then on the bottom in every pixel I'm actually gonna encode the steps to
reproduce the drawing until the progress bar fills up and then I'm gonna store
these steps in the image but also these numbers are gonna be stored on chain so
I'm gonna have redundancy because visually these are decodable into the
steps so there's gonna be a player program that can load the images and
play them back and also the data for that is gonna be stored on chain too so
even if you don't have the image but you just have the chain data you can use
the player to reconstruct the image

videos.desc: Reply to @xerion256
videos.text: I see, so you're looking at the whistle graphs and you're thinking there's like an x-axis and a y-axis, and then up here the notes have to be high, and down here the notes have to be low, and then if you were to take a whistle graph like this one, I'm a butterfly flapping for you guys, it's just a costume I put on in my room, then somehow you should be able to map this onto like a pitch grid, but that's not what whistle graphing is about because the sounds are totally arbitrary with the lines, and it's all about what happens in the imagination when you compose different sounds with different lines.

segments.text: I see, so you're looking at the whistle graphs and you're thinking there's like an x-axis
and a y-axis, and then up here the notes have to be high, and down here the notes have to
be low, and then if you were to take a whistle graph like this one, I'm a butterfly flapping
for you guys, it's just a costume I put on in my room, then somehow you should be able
to map this onto like a pitch grid, but that's not what whistle graphing is about because
the sounds are totally arbitrary with the lines, and it's all about what happens in the imagination
when you compose different sounds with different lines.

videos.desc: Reply to @yojessechu  what’s your apple style? 🍎
videos.text: When Camille, Jeffrey, and I are first teaching each other a whistle graph. We do it in this very clean robotic way like Hey there, Apple. Are you okay? videos.desc: Reply to @jamesdowns15 see what i mean?
videos.text: this comment is saying that because I said that whistle graphs have arbitrary connections between the marks that you make and the sounds that you make that there's no logic to it, which isn't exactly true. So just because on a whistle graph you can do something like this, when you're told amazing, incredible, and the words that I'm saying don't necessarily have anything to do with high pitch being up here and low pitch being down there or, you know, I'm not on a Cartesian XY grid. It's not a geometric logic, but it is a gestural logic, right? So listen to this. When you're told amazing, incredible, see how the gestures of the lines map to the syllables of the words? That's pretty much the logic of whistle graphing that you have to pay attention to.

videos.desc: Replying to @leo412a one of the points is making poetry that you might find relevant
videos.text: And that was when I looked in at my phone. I mean, there really isn't a point, but there is something that makes certain whistle graphs good or bad, and it's the same thing that makes certain kinds of music good or bad, and it's really just how much it matters to people. And, you know, that changes and what matters changes, but folk music is really just a record of all the things that a group of people in history cared about enough to share and keep spreading. So if a whistle graph gets stuck in your mind, then I guess that's part of the point.

videos.text: ByteDance is basically a multinational corporation. It was started in China, but it's definitely Chinese and definitely has some CCP government shit going on. They run this thing called Dooyin. Dooyin is basically the YouTube of China. China doesn't have Meta, China doesn't have Google, they do have some Apple stuff. They don't have these social networks, but they have Dooyin and Dooyin is fucking crazy. It's light years ahead of TikTok. They also have TikTok. Both these have different app client code bases that are probably, we're shared at some point. Now, how did TikTok start? TikTok started because an ex-ByteDance employee went ahead, came to America, started an app called Musically and then later sold that app called TikTok back to the parent Chinese multinational corporation. Now, here's where it gets tricky because the source code of the back end is fucking shared, you guys. It's shared by both TikTok and by Dooyin and ByteDance has got the repos. So basically, here's what happens. All of a sudden, last year, two engineers, two people from ByteDance in China, they doxxed Americans. So they actually looked at American data in China and they doxxed American journalists and this became a news story. So that's why all this stuff is being triggered because essentially the machine learning models and the back end data is actually shared among these networks. And right now, when you're for a YouTube page, you're basically seeing stuff that says like, "Oh, let's keep TikTok, let's keep it, let's keep it." But what makes you think that, that's not actually being pushed out on purpose, potentially by ByteDance because maybe they want Americans to get upset. Maybe they want Americans to be like, "Oh, government bad, government take away TikTok." Maybe it's part of an entire psychological game where they want to capture an entire voting generation of Americans and make them kind of pro-Chinese, you know? And maybe even Apple's involved 'cause Apple loves China, dude.

videos.desc: Replying to @mattmgk here’s one way to consider it practically and conceptually #artist #creator #drawing
videos.text: Hi this is Jeffrey the creator of whistlegraph and I can explain to you what it is. Now a while back I wanted to figure out a way to unload graphics. Unload some some graphics out of my brain and onto a page and whenever I unloaded those graphics like a graphics file you know like if you have a PNG file you unload that off of the human brain onto a page and what I was trying to figure out is how can I do that nicely how can I memorize graphics. So what I came up with was a musical scheme and you know we basically every line has a sound associated with it and in the end you have this poetic image. Butterfly fly bing for you guys it's just a costume I put on in my room and those Those steps all come together into an art form.

videos.desc: rehearsing and making boxes #graphics #drawing
videos.text: Hey guys, this is Jeffrey from Whistlegraph and I'm here with Camille and Alex and we're in Green Point practicing one of our favorite pieces called Making Boxes. We've been rehearsing this piece for you guys for about five hours now and we're definitely excited to show it to you. These are some of our previous rehearsals. Are you ready for one? Yeah. Let's do one right here. Making Boxes, Making Boxes, Little Boxes, Bigger Boxes, Bigger Boxes, Bigger Boxes, Bigger Boxes, Bigger Boxes now. Yeah that was a pretty well practiced one. I think we can do better. Let's do another. We're going to do one more for you. Ready? Making Boxes, Making Boxes, Little Boxes, Bigger Boxes, Bigger Boxes, Bigger Boxes, Bigger Boxes, Barely Boxes now. Wow!

videos.desc: three whistlegraphs 🙂 #drawing #doodle #fountainpen #performance
videos.text: It's a beautiful day to perform three whistle graphs for you guys. Number one, I'm going to do a sad campfire. It's chilly, but I keep burning For people who aren't around anymore All alone Somebody stomp me out of my misery Number two, I don't need an iPhone. Fun fact, Camille, Jeffrey, and I actually wrote this one together. It was the first three person whistle graph, and it goes like this. I don't need an iPhone I can take your picture In between my fingers I don't need an iPhone And the third whistle graph today is a very scary looking sad mushroom But I'm going to make it cute. I'm going to give it a smile. It's going to be the first smiling mushroom on the account. It goes like this. Keep me in this dark place Because I like it here. Hope you enjoyed three whistle graphs.

segments.text: It's a beautiful day to perform three whistle graphs for you guys.

I don't need an iPhone
I can take your picture
In between my fingers
I don't need an iPhone

But I'm going to make it cute. I'm going to give it a smile.
It's going to be the first smiling mushroom on the account.
It goes like this.
Keep me in this dark place
Because I like it here.

videos.desc: Replying to @RailofRoads yes i miss them
videos.text: I'm pretty sad about Camille and Alex not being around anymore, and it's been a little over two months now since we sort of started to have a big break from each other. Collaboration is hard, and our collaboration started to sort of show its cracks and become a lot more difficult over a year ago actually, but despite that we kept making work and kept trying to make good work and did make good work, but you know everything kind of needs to come to some kind of end at some point. In terms of sad whistle graphs, also a lot of my whistle graphs have always been sad, and there's a sadness inside of humanity that's always been there, and there's a sadness inside of every person, and unless you give that sadness a shape and give a voice to that sadness somehow, then you're going to be alone in that, and that's really what whistle graphing is for. That's what all art is for. It's for externalizing those feelings, and that's something to celebrate.

videos.desc: feelings about primitive geometric boundaries #circle #triangle #square
videos.text: Did you ever think about how being inside of a circle, being trapped inside of a circle, would be really scary? Because no matter where you go, you're kind of like in the same exact spot. There's no way to differentiate. But then being trapped inside of a square would be a little bit better because there's kind of four places to lose yourself in. But then the worst of all is being trapped inside of a triangle. Because no matter where you look, there's two fucking things right behind you.

videos.desc: just good penmanship tips
videos.text: It's important for professional whistle graffers to always carry a couple of different pins. So if you're entertaining a kitty cat, then you want to be able to do something like draw a really nice cat that's going to show up nice and dark for everybody. So the first color you want to have is a dark color like this. Then you always want to have a red color, isn't that right? Yeah, a red color. And that helps with adding blood to the kitty cat. And this makes things a little bit spookier. Now, one thing you always want to do to keep them on their toes is you want to go around the back with the bloodstream. And right on the back of the kitty cat's head, you want to add a heart. That way, they'll never give up hope.

videos.desc: i guess forgot how to breathe like a baby
videos.text: Hi, I'm posting this video as a kind of a time capsule video to follow my weird body noise issues that I started having a year and a half ago in my hand. And I realized very recently that I only ever breathe through my chest like... And I was reading online that people aren't really supposed to breathe that way and when you watch a baby breathe they just breathe through their diaphragm like this deeper way. And so I started practicing that and I don't know how long I've been breathing just out of my chest probably like 10 or 15 years maybe more but here's what happens when I just breathe with my diaphragm. I'll show you. You can hear all the noises and stuff in my back and before I was like forcing those noises but honestly I've been practicing breathing like this for five hours today and it's awesome and I feel I mean it's kind of painful but I also feel a lot better weirdly and I've been reading a lot of information online about how your body stores trauma and stuff like that and yeah I probably just have a lot of that shit inside me so anyway I'll make another video in like a few months and see if these noises go away but I'm gonna keep practicing practicing this every day.
